macro_segments.title: Introduction and Overview: Learning the Prohibition of Eating Meat and Milk from Forbidden Cooking

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and scope of discussion on Daf 114-115

macro_segments.title: Introduction and Overview: Learning the Prohibition of Eating Meat and Milk from Forbidden Cooking (continued)

micro_segments.title: The problem: How do we know meat and milk is forbidden to eat when the verse only mentions cooking?

macro_segments.title: First Approach: Rav Ashi's Teaching - Learning from 'Kol To'evah' (All Abominations)

micro_segments.title: Rav Ashi's derivation: The product of a forbidden act (cooking meat and milk) is itself an abomination
Establishing that 'Lo Tochal' (do not eat) includes prohibition of benefit through Rabbi Abbahu's principle
The Baraita about Neveilah (non-kosher carcass): how 'do not eat' extends to benefit through the explicit exception
Dispute between Rabbi Meir and Rabbi Yehudah on whether you can give neveilah to both ger and non-Jew or separately
Tosafot's point: The neveilah exception applies only because monetary benefit would be trivial

macro_segments.title: Challenges to the 'Product of Forbidden Act' Model: Why Certain Prohibited Acts Don't Forbid Their Products

micro_segments.title: Rashi's defense: Meat and milk is uniquely the direct embodiment of the forbidden act of cooking
Why Kilayim (forbidden mixtures) are permitted: Learning from the case of mixed seeds versus mixed vines
Other prohibited acts with permissible products: Threshing with mixed animals and muzzling
The case of mother and child slaughter on same day: Why is the meat permissible?
The case of sending away the mother bird: Why doesn't the obligation make the bird forbidden?

macro_segments.title: Second Approach: Reish Lakish's Teaching - Learning from the Pesach Korban and the Double Language of Cooking

micro_segments.title: Reish Lakish's derivation: The double language 'vashel mevushal' (cooked, cooked) in the Pesach law alludes to meat and milk
Rabbi Yochanan's challenge: Isn't Rabbi's derasha from Pesulei Mukdashin better?
Rabbi's derasha from Pesulei Mukdashin: Context of hybrid animals and learning about meat and milk

macro_segments.title: Third and Fourth Approaches: From Neveilah Laws and the Concept of Kedusha (Holiness)

micro_segments.title: Dvei Rabbi Eliezer's derasha: From the neveilah law, inference that meat and milk cooked is forbidden in benefit
Rabbi Yishmael's approach: The three-fold repetition of the verse provides three prohibitions: cooking, eating, and benefit
Issi ben Yehudah's derasha: Learning from 'Am Kadosh' (holy people) that meat and milk is forbidden in eating

macro_segments.title: The Kal V'Chomer (Fortiori Argument) for Benefit Prohibition and Three-Case Rule

micro_segments.title: Setting up the kal v'chomer: If orlah (never permitted) is forbidden in benefit, meat and milk should be too
Objection: But things where aveirah was done are often permitted—wheat threshed with mixed animals, muzzled cow
Rav Ashi's response using Chometz B'Pesach: Things can become forbidden in benefit even if once permitted
Introduction of Kilayim Ha-Kerem as third case: Why three cases are needed to establish a binding rule
The logical principle: With two cases you can dismiss trivially; with three you need substantial reason

topical_tags.term: Product of Forbidden Act (Embodiment of Aveirah)
